title: Te Rongoā o Te Awa: Healing Practices of Mangakōtukutuku Stream / The Healing Wisdom of Our Waters
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: Students will explore traditional Māori healing practices (rongoā) through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our college lands. This awa has been a source of healing, sustenance, and spiritual connection for Ngāti Kahungunu for generations. We will connect with local kaumātua who maintain knowledge of specific rongoā rākau (medicinal plants) that grow along its banks and their traditional uses for physical and spiritual wellbeing.
learningObjectives: Identify and document at least three rongoā rākau found along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and explain their traditional medicinal uses
Create a personal wellbeing plan incorporating both traditional Māori practices and contemporary health knowledge
Demonstrate proper tikanga (protocols) when gathering and preparing rongoā materials under guidance of local experts
Analyze how connection to whenua (land) and awa (waterways) contributes to holistic hauora (wellbeing)

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga: Connecting to Our Awa
activities.description: Guided walk along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua, learning the history and significance of the waterway while identifying native plants with medicinal properties

activities.title: Rongoā Rākau Discovery
activities.description: Hands-on workshop where students learn to identify, harvest (with proper karakia and tikanga), and prepare basic rongoā such as kawakawa balms or harakeke infusions

activities.title: Hauora Wheel Integration
activities.description: Students create personal hauora wheels that integrate traditional Māori healing practices with contemporary health knowledge, reflecting on how both contribute to taha tinana, taha wairua, taha hinengaro, and taha whānau

activities.title: Healing Narratives
activities.description: Interview local community members about their experiences with rongoā Māori and create digital stories that preserve and share this knowledge

activities.title: Kaitiakitanga in Action
activities.description: Practical conservation activity along the awa - planting native rongoā species and learning about sustainable harvesting practices to ensure these resources remain for future generations

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and rongoā practitioners from local marae
DOC website: 'Traditional Use of Native Plants' resources
Book: 'Rongoā Māori: A Practical Guide to Traditional Māori Medicine' by Rob McGowan
Mangakōtukutuku College's environmental monitoring equipment for water quality testing

tasks: Create an illustrated guide to three rongoā plants from our awa with traditional uses and preparation methods
Develop and present a personal hauora plan that integrates at least one traditional practice
Participate in a group reflection on the connection between environmental health and human wellbeing

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A1 - Personal Health and Physical Development
Health and Physical Education: C1 - Relationships with Other People
Health and Physical Education: D1 - Healthy Communities and Environments
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways
